questions.scenario:
`devpolaris-orders-api` writes this log after checkout:

```text
receiptId=rcpt_ord_8841 contentType=application/pdf bytes=734812
ownerCustomerId=cus_77 downloadLater=true
```

The database already has the order row and only needs to remember where the file lives. Which service shape best fits the receipt bytes?

options.text: Store receipt bytes in RDS so the order transaction and file lifecycle share one consistency boundary.
S3, because the receipt is a whole object that can be stored by bucket and key.
Store receipt metadata in DynamoDB and the binary payload as a base64 attribute for exact-key reads.
EFS, because several API tasks can mount the receipt path and serve the PDF through shared filesystem access.

questions.explanation:
The PDF is file-shaped data: the app writes the whole object and later reads the whole object. S3 fits that promise, while RDS should keep the ownership and pointer metadata. Storing the PDF bytes in RDS or on one server disk would make the database or server carry file delivery work it does not need.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 object keys](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-keys.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris is adding durable receipt downloads to the orders API. The order transaction already commits in RDS, and the generated PDF must survive task replacement and remain tied to the authorized order.

Order write is create the order in RDS, then generate the receipt PDF. RDS row is `orderId=ord_1042 customerId=cus_77 receiptKey=null`. Receipt is 734812 bytes with content type `application/pdf`.

What is the cleanest design after the receipt is generated?

options.text: Write the PDF to S3 before the order transaction, then store its key whether or not the order commits.
Commit the order and upload the receipt, but mark receipt state complete before verifying the S3 write.
Upload the PDF to S3 and update the RDS row with the S3 key after authorization metadata is clear.
Store the PDF in RDS first, then migrate older files to S3 after the table reaches a size threshold.

questions.explanation:
This split keeps each service doing its natural job. RDS stores the business record and ownership, while S3 stores the durable receipt object. Putting large file bytes into RDS or a task-local path makes later downloads and recovery harder.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 object keys](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-keys.html).

questions.scenario:
A retry storm produces this DynamoDB log:

```text
event=checkout.idempotency_duplicate
awsError=ConditionalCheckFailedException
idempotencyKey=checkout_01HW2S8RN8Z7
requestHash=sha256:18f9b31c
existingStatus=processing
```

How should the on-call engineer interpret it?

options.text: Treat the conditional failure as a duplicate and return success without checking whether the existing item is still processing or failed.
Increase table capacity because conditional failures consume the same diagnosis path as throttled writes.
It may be the correct safety behavior, so the app should read the existing item and return the current job or result.
Change the condition to an unconditional put so the latest retry becomes authoritative.

questions.explanation:
For idempotency, a conditional check failure can mean another request already claimed the key. That is not automatically an outage. Removing the condition would allow duplicate work, which is exactly what the check prevents.

Learn more in [DynamoDB condition expressions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris needs to place four kinds of data for the orders workload: final order and payment records that require transactions, receipt PDFs downloaded later, checkout idempotency records read by exact key, and temporary report chunks used by one worker before the final archive is uploaded. Which three service pairings are reasonable first choices?

options.text: Receipt PDF objects in S3, with the order row storing the object key.
Temporary report chunks on EFS as the durable source of truth after the producing worker exits.
Final order and payment records in RDS.
Checkout idempotency records in DynamoDB when the access pattern is exact-key lookup.
Receipt PDF objects on EFS because browser downloads require filesystem paths shared by every API task.

questions.explanation:
The first three pairings match the data shape: relational records in RDS, file-shaped objects in S3, and known-key operational state in DynamoDB. The EBS option is tempting when a worker is already involved, but an attached disk is not a safe source of truth for checkout records.

Learn more in [DynamoDB NoSQL design](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/bp-general-nosql-design.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris is designing a vendor-file workflow for several workers. The workflow currently depends on a shared mounted path and filesystem operations.

Name is vendor input processing. Data is large input files from a private transfer process. Read path is several workers need open, read, rename, and directory listing on the same tree. Path is /mnt/devpolaris-input/incoming.

Which question best decides between EFS and S3 plus queue processing?

options.text: Choose EFS from the current path syntax without testing whether workers can process immutable objects independently.
Do workers truly need shared filesystem semantics, or can each worker process a named object independently?
Choose S3 plus SQS even if the application still requires shared rename and directory-listing semantics.
Choose EFS from one mount-target plan without checking access from each worker Availability Zone.

questions.explanation:
EFS is useful when existing tools need a shared mounted directory. If the workflow can pass object keys through a queue, S3 may be simpler and more durable. The right choice depends on file semantics, not on the presence of a path string.

Learn more in [Amazon EFS concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/whatisefs.html).

questions.scenario: A DevPolaris report job writes disposable chunks under `/tmp`, builds one archive, uploads the archive to `s3://devpolaris-orders-prod-objects/exports/2026/05/report.zip`, and then records the output key as complete. The worker may be replaced between attempts, so the workflow must distinguish temporary work from the durable result. Which statement is most accurate?

options.text: Keep temporary chunks local but mark the job complete before verifying the final S3 upload.
Upload each chunk as the durable report and derive job completion from the number of objects under the prefix.
Record job completion before upload so retries can skip rebuilding the archive.
Temporary local chunks are acceptable only if the job can retry and the durable result is confirmed elsewhere.

questions.explanation:
Scratch space is fine when the data is disposable and the final output is durable. The job still needs a record of completion so the UI and support can distinguish done, failed, and still running. S3 stores the object, but it does not replace all workflow state.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 object keys](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-keys.html).

questions.scenario:
The receipt route logs:

```text
route=GET /orders/ord_1042/receipt
query=select receipt_blob from orders where id=$1
receipt_bytes=734812
duration_ms=1840
```

What wrong-fit pattern does this suggest?

options.text: RDS is carrying file bytes that would be better stored as S3 objects with keys in the database.
Keep the PDF in RDS but add a read replica so file downloads do not affect the writer.
Move the PDF to S3 but omit the object key and ownership metadata from the relational order record.
Move the complete order record and receipt into S3 so one storage service owns the feature.

questions.explanation:
The database can store bytes, but that does not make it the right file delivery layer. Receipt PDFs are durable objects, while the database should keep ownership and pointer metadata. This is the opposite wrong fit from using S3 as an order database.

Learn more in [Amazon RDS DB instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/Overview.DBInstance.html).
